paper_title: A Matter of TASTE: Improving Coverage and Difficulty of Agent Benchmarks

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: As agent capabilities advance, existing benchmarks, such as $τ^2$-Bench, are becoming increasingly saturated. Yet constructing new benchmark tasks remains complex, costly, and labor-intensive. Moreover, the standard approach, in which scenarios are first written in natural language and then mapped to tool sequences, captures only a narrow subset of the tool-use patterns agents exercise. In this paper, we address these problems by reversing the task construction process. We propose TASTE: Task Synthesis from Tool Sequence Evolution, an automatic method that generates challenging tasks with broader tool-use coverage. TASTE utilizes an Adaptive Contrastive $n$-gram model trained on LLM-judged validity signals. This enables sampling valid tool sequences that cover a vast range of tool combinations. TASTE then selects representative sequences from the pool via clustering, instantiates them into complete benchmark tasks, and refines them through iterative difficulty evolution. Using TASTE, we construct $τ^c$-Bench, a challenging extension of the three domains of $τ^2$-Bench. We evaluate $11$ agent/user LLM pairs and find that models nearly saturating $τ^2$-Bench suffer severe performance drops on our tasks (e.g., Gemini-3-Flash falls from $0.82\!-\!0.94$ to $0.28\!-\!0.61$). Beyond increasing difficulty, our generated tasks more than double the number of unique tool combinations agents must execute. Our results suggest high scores on existing benchmarks often reflect saturation rather than robust task-solving ability. By automating the generation of difficult, high-coverage benchmarks, TASTE enables continuous, scalable evaluation of future agents.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Tool-using agents interact with and modify external environments through tool invocations [1], such as executing code [2, 3], legal complaint drafting, managing customer accounts, or placing orders [46]. Unlike standard input-output systems, tool-using agents operate through multi-step actions that 1The code for TASTE and the τ c-Bench data are available at Anonymous Git Repo. Preprint. Figure 1: Illustration of TASTE, our three-stage method for synthetic task generation. We demonstrate the method across the three domains of τ 2-Bench (Verified, τ BV). Evaluating Gemini-3-Flash agent (averaged over two different user LLMs) shows that, unlike τ BV, τ c-Bench is non-saturated. Besides being more challenging, τ c-Bench exhibits broader coverage, requiring agents to exercise more diverse set of tool combinations, as quantified by the type-token ratio (TTR; see 5). evolve the world state, making evaluation dependent not on any single response but on the cumulative effect of the entire interaction [7]. natural evaluation paradigm, adopted by benchmarks such as τ -Bench [8, 9], is final-state evaluation: task is solved if the world state at termination matches target state induced by gold tool-call sequence. Constructing benchmarks under this paradigm is particularly demanding. Authoring even single task involves writing natural-language instructions, configuring the environment, deriving the correct sequence of tool calls, and verifying that the target state is both consistent and reachable [10, 11]. At the same time, agent capabilities are advancing rapidly, and leading models are approaching the ceiling of existing benchmarks [79, 1215]. The combination of costly manual construction and accelerating saturation creates an urgent need for automatic benchmark generation [16, 17]. However, effective automation first requires identifying the properties good benchmark should satisfy and how to measure them. Our paper begins by proposing three desiderata for benchmarks of tool-using agents. Validity requires every task to be both automatically verifiable and correct in that the gold final state is reachable from the task specification. Difficulty requires tasks to meaningfully separate agents of different capability levels. Coverage requires benchmark tasks to span structurally diverse patterns of tool use rather than oversampling redundant scenarios. Among these three, coverage has received the least attention and lacks clear operationalization [18]. In this paper, we define it through the lens of tool sequences: the ordered list of tool names in the gold trajectory leading to the target state. Framing coverage in terms of tool sequences also opens constructive pathway: if the space of valid tool sequences can be explored and sampled directly, it becomes possible to design an automatic method for task generation that explicitly targets all three desiderata. We propose to reverse the task construction process entirely. The prevailing approach involves an author who first writes natural-language scenario and then derives the corresponding tool sequence. Because the tool-sequence space is never explicitly explored, the resulting benchmarks exercise only arbitrary tool combinations that vary with author choice [18]. Accordingly, rather than starting from scenarios and recovering tool sequences, we sample diverse set of tool sequences and synthesize the full tasks around them. We instantiate this idea and propose TASTE (Task Synthesis from Tool Sequence Evolution), an automatic method for task generation comprising three stages, each targeting one or more of these desiderata. TASTE is illustrated in Figure 1. In the first stage, we train an Adaptive Contrastive n-gram model over the space of tool sequences. The model maintains separate count tables for n-grams (units are tools) observed in plausibly valid and implausibly valid sequences, as judged by an LLM, and computes sampling probabilities 2 from their contrastive ratio. Counts are updated online through iterative sample-and-validate loops, progressively refining the distribution. This targets validity by concentrating probability mass on valid tool sequences, and coverage by exploring regions of the tool-combination space. The second stage selects representative subset of (the benchmark size) sequences from the sampled pool using K-medoids clustering [19]. We employ weighted Levenshtein distance that reflects semantic and functional similarity between tools. In the third stage, each selected tool sequence is instantiated into complete benchmark task (user instruction, database records, tool arguments). Following rule-based checks, we employ hint-assisted verifier agent to ensure the task is actually solvable: we provide corrupted version of the gold tool-call sequence (tools are shuffled, arguments are masked), requiring the agent to cooperate with the simulated user to reach the target state. final evolution step targets interaction difficulty: making the user ambiguous and less cooperative, and introducing confusing decoy records to the database (e.g., fully booked flight on the desired route). Using TASTE, we construct τ c-Bench, challenging extension of the three domains of τ 2-Bench [9]: Airline, Retail, and Telecom (K = 50/114/114 respectively). We then evaluate 11 agent/user LLM pairs across all three domains. We find performance on our τ c-Bench is consistently and substantially lower than on the original τ 2-Bench, with agents scoring up to 80% worse. The degradation is particularly striking for models that appear to have nearly saturated the τ 2-Bench: Gemini-3-Flash achieves 0.820.94 on the original yet drops to 0.280.61 on ours. Beyond increased difficulty, our benchmark more than doubles the number of unique tool combinations agents must execute: the weighted edit distance between sequences rises by up to 124%, the type-token ratio (TTR, the proportion of unique n-grams) by up to 111%, and tool-frequency entropy by 35%. These results suggest that high scores on existing benchmarks often reflect saturation rather than robust task-solving ability, underscoring the need for automated methods like τ c-Bench that can continuously generate harder tasks with broader coverage. To summarize, our contributions are as follows: (1) We articulate three desiderata for benchmarks of tool-using agents: validity, difficulty, and coverage. We further operationalize coverage through tool sequences. (2) We propose TASTE, method for automatic task generation that enables exploration of the tool-sequence space. (3) We construct τ c-Bench, challenging extension of τ 2-Bench, evaluate it on 11 agent/user LLM pairs, and demonstrate that τ c-Bench is harder and provides broader coverage of tool-use patterns.

sections.title: 2 Evaluating Conversational Tool-Using Agents
sections.content: We focus on conversational tool-using agents, particularly complex and interesting special case of general tool use, in which static, single-turn prompts are replaced by continuous interactions between two agents. This setup naturally subsumes single-turn scenarios. Our setup involves conversation between two agents: the evaluated agent and the simulated user. The interaction begins with the evaluated agent receiving task. At each turn, an agent takes an action, either sending message to the other agent or performing tool call. If the action is sending message, the turn passes to the other agent. If the action is tool call, the environment may modify the world state or return an observation, visible only to the calling agent, after which the agent continues its turn. This setup makes evaluation fundamentally more challenging than in standard input-output systems. Evaluation must be carried out over trajectories of actions that are shaped jointly by the agents and the environment, and also evolve the world state [7]. As result, correctness must be determined by the resulting world state. natural solution adopted by the τ -Bench benchmark [8] is final-state evaluation:2 task is solved if the interaction terminates in world state equivalent to predefined target state, induced by gold tool-call sequence. Accordingly, many different trajectories may be correct, as long as they lead to this target state. Formally, let denote the environment, which comprises the domain policy (e.g., business logic, guidelines) state space, the set of available tools , and an execution engine that operationalizes these tools. tool call is an action that invokes tool with arguments: = t(**kwargs). task instance is defined as = (s0, u, σ), where s0 is the initial state (e.g., database), is the user instruction, and σ = (a n) is gold (but not necessarily unique) tool-call sequence. The 1, . . . , 2Final-state evaluation reduces assessment to single binary check on the end state, overlooking trajectory-level properties such as efficiency, intermediate rewards, and interaction quality. τ -Bench supports additional rewards, such as action checks or communication checks, but in practice these are excluded from the main reward in most tasks. We adopt the final-state reward alone, both for simplicity and for consistency with how results are standardly reported in τ -Bench evaluations. 3 target final state is then derived as = apply(σ, s0). Let σ = (a1, . . . , ak) denote the tool-call sequence produced during the interaction, i.e., the ordered list of all tool calls made by both agents. The reward is defined based on the state induced by σ and s: rx(σ) = 1[apply(σ, s0) s]. Finally, we distinguish between tool-call sequence σ and its corresponding tool sequence σ = (t1, . . . , tk) k, which is its projection onto the tool-identifiers space, that is, the ordered list of which tools were called, stripped of their arguments. In particular, σ denotes the gold tool sequence. σ is central concept in our study, as it captures the structural requirements of task and directly connects to the coverage and difficulty properties of benchmark.

sections.title: 2.1 Desiderata for Agent Benchmarks
sections.content: We next propose three properties that characterize the quality of benchmark (i.e., set of tasks) for tool-using agents. Validity. task is valid if it is both verifiable and correct. Verifiability means that task success can be automatically checked (e.g., via final-state evaluation) rather than relying on subjective judgments. Correctness means that the gold final state is consistent and faithful with the task specification (s0, u), i.e., it can be achieved given s0 and user instructed with u. Without high proportion of valid tasks, agents may be penalized for benchmark defects rather than true capability limitations [11]. Indeed, [10] identified dozens of invalid tasks in τ -Bench, which were corrected in τ -Bench Verified. Coverage. useful benchmark should cover the relevant task space, rather than sampling similar tasks for smaller subspace. In our setting, each task is associated with gold tool sequence σ (i.e., the names of the tools leading to s). We define coverage in terms of these gold tool sequences, thereby abstracting away from superficial linguistic variation in user instructions and initial states, and focusing instead on the procedural structure of the task. benchmark has good coverage if its tasks represent structurally different tool sequences, rather than many near-duplicates. In addition, benchmark tasks should reflect common patterns of tool use, rather than arbitrary combinations or isolated outliers. Coverage can be quantified, for example, using the tool-edit distance between sequences or the number of unique n-grams of tools. This mirrors standard diversity/novelty quantification in NLP [20], with the key distinction that our units are tool names rather than characters or words. Difficulty. benchmark should contain tasks that meaningfully differentiate between capability levels. If all tasks are too easy, the benchmark quickly saturates; if all tasks are too hard, it cannot provide useful ranking of agents. In our setting, task difficulty arises from two sources. structural difficulty, determined by properties of the gold tool sequence, such as its length and the types of tools involved (e.g., write operations versus simple reads), and interaction difficulty, determined by the instruction ambiguity, initial-state complexity (e.g., distractors, dependencies, stale information), and user behavior (e.g., cooperative vs. adversarial).

sections.title: 3 TASTE: Task Synthesis from Tool Sequence Evolution
sections.content: We next propose TASTE, synthetic data generation pipeline for constructing new tasks within given environment. TASTE is designed to satisfy the three properties defined in Section 2.1, enabling fully automated task generation without costly manual curation or correction. It operates around tool sequences, and proceeds in three stages, as illustrated in Figure 1: 1. Tool Sequence Sampling (targets validity and coverage): We propose novel sampling method based on an Adaptive Contrastive n-gram language model, which is trained to model the distribution of plausible tool sequences. The model samples thousands of diverse candidate sequences under desired constraints, such as the distribution of sequence lengths. 2. Clustering and Selection (targets coverage): We cluster candidate tool sequences using K-medoids with semantically weighted Levenshtein distance. This yields internally coherent clusters of sequences with similar procedural structure, while medoids across clusters capture structurally distinct patterns. 3. Task Generation and Evolution (targets validity and difficulty): Each selected sequence (medoid) is instantiated as complete benchmark task and validated through rule-based 4 checks and hint-assisted verifier agent. Once complete task is obtained, we apply difficulty evolution that progressively increases task complexity.

sections.title: 3.1 Tool Sequence Sampling with an Adaptive Contrastive n-gram Model
sections.content: To address coverage, this stage produces large and diverse pool of candidate tool sequences, from which representative sequences are later selected. We use an n-gram language model, in which the probability of the i-th tool depends only on the preceding n1 tools. We call this model the Adaptive Contrastive n-gram model, as it extends the standard n-gram model in two ways. Adaptive: the n-gram probabilities are updated online as sequences are sampled and validated. Contrastive: the model maintains separate count tables for n-grams observed in plausibly valid and implausibly valid sequences, and computes sampling probabilities from their ratio. Model. Let ctxi = (tin+1, . . . , ti1) denote the context of length n1 preceding position i. We maintain two n-gram count tables, + and , constructed from plausible and implausible sequences, respectively. Each table defines conditional score over the next tool, given the context. The conditional probability of sampling the i-th tool given ctxi is defined via contrastive ratio: S(ti ctxi) = (ctxi, ti) + λ0 tT (ctxi, t) + λ0 (cid:80) , (ti ctxi) (cid:32) S+(ti ctxi) S(ti ctxi)λneg (cid:33)T (k) . where λ0 > 0 is Dirichlet smoothing parameter, λneg 0 controls the influence of negative evidence, and (k) is temperature that exponentially decays over training iterations. Adaptive Training. The model is trained via an iterative generate-and-validate loop that progressively refines the distribution over tool sequences. We initialize the count tables from seed set of existing tool sequences. At each iteration, we sample candidate sequences autoregressively from the current model, generating each tool conditioned on the preceding n1 tools. Each sampled sequence is validated by prompted LLM, which assigns binary plausibility label indicating whether the sequence could arise as solution to realistic scenario (Box D.1). This label serves as the learning signal: n-grams from plausible sequences update +; those from implausible ones update . Rationale. Our design balances coverage and validity. We use an n-gram model because it is simple, scalable, and well-suited to tool sequences, where many constraints are local: the plausibility of the next tool often depends on the tools executed recently. Concretely, our sampler is temperatureannealed log-odds between two Dirichlet-smoothed n-gram models over plausible and implausible sequences, trained online via LLM-judged pseudo-labels. The contrastive formulation uses both positive and negative evidence, favoring n-grams observed in plausible sequences while penalizing those associated with implausible ones. Negative evidence is especially useful (Figure 3) because some local patterns strongly indicate invalidity, e.g., modifying reservation after canceling it. Adaptive online training is needed (Figure 3) because the tool-sequence space is combinatorially large and often only small supervised set is available.

sections.title: 3.2 Clustering and Selection of Representative Tool Sequences
sections.content: At this stage, we can sample candidate tool sequences at scale, enabling us to generate large and diverse pool of candidates from the trained model, where is the target benchmark size (N = 2000, {50, 114}). We control sequence lengths by drawing from configurable distribution (a skew-normal distribution with µ = 7, maximum length of 15) and enforce uniqueness through resampling. From this pool, we select representative tool sequences for task generation. Clustering. We use K-medoids clustering [21, 19], which partitions the pool into clusters and selects representative medoid from each. The algorithm minimizes the total distance between each medoid and its cluster members, so each medoid represents group of similar candidates. The pool provides diversity; medoid selection ensures that central sequences are chosen rather than outliers. Distance between Tool Sequences. Using the standard Levenshtein (edit) distance, which assigns unit cost to all operations, led to unintuitive clusters: sequences that differ in semantically 5 important ways were often grouped together. We therefore employ weighted variant of the Levenshtein distance, in which substitution costs depend on the semantic relationship between tools. Concretely, replacing tools that perform similar function (e.g., search_direct_flight and search_onestop_flight) costs 0.33; replacing tools of the same type (e.g., both read or both write) cost 0.66; and substitutions across types, insertions, and deletions cost 1 (Appendix B). qualitative analysis of clusters and medoids constructed with standard and weighted error distances is provided in Appendix A. As shown, clusters formed under the weighted distance are more coherent, with sequences within each cluster having similar functional structure. Medoids Validation. We validate medoids using the plausibility LLM validator from the n-gram training stage. We apply the following recovery procedure when medoid is deemed invalid: Replace the invalid medoid with the next-closest member of the same cluster. If all members of cluster are invalid, remove them from the pool and re-run K-medoids with all currently valid medoids fixed as constraints. Repeat until all medoids are valid.

sections.title: 3.3 Generating Tasks from Tool Sequence and Evolving the Difficulty
sections.content: Given set of representative tool sequences, we proceed in two stages: (i) generating verifiable and correct base task for each sequence, and (ii) evolving the task to increase difficulty while preserving its correctness. Base Task Generation. Each tool sequence σ is instantiated into complete, executable task by generating s0 and u. This requires two LLM calls. First, the LLM constructs coherent real-world scenario with respect to the existing environment E, and that motivates σ, inventing concrete entities (e.g., user names, order IDs) and producing verbose, step-by-step user instruction (Box D.2). Second, follow-up call generates the database entities required to execute the task (Box D.3). This process yields base task: an intentionally unambiguous instantiation designed to isolate task validity from difficulty (Example 6) Validity Checks. We validate each generated task using combination of deterministic and simulation-based checks. First, we apply deterministic rule-based checks to verify basic structural validity and ensure that the gold tool-call sequence executes successfully (e.g., that referenced entities exist in s0 and that tool calls conform to the domain schema). These checks are fast and catch structural errors, but they do not ensure that the task is solvable or that the user instruction contains sufficient information. We therefore perform simulation-based solvability check using Verifier agent. The agent attempts to solve through interaction with the user, while receiving partial hints derived from the gold tool-call sequence: the tool calls are shuffled, and for each call, kwargs arguments are removed. Since these hints are incomplete and unordered, they help guide the agent without allowing it to simply replay the solution. The agent must still infer the intended plan, recover missing arguments, and cooperate with the user; success, therefore, provides evidence that the task is solvable. If the generated task fails any check, it is regenerated; after repeated failures, the gold tool-call sequence is replaced by another candidate from the same cluster (Section 3.2). Task Evolution. The generated base tasks do not reflect realistic conditions: real-world user requests are ambiguous, changing over time, and sometimes adversarial, and the environment may contain confusing or misleading instances. The evolution stage transforms base tasks into harder variants while preserving the gold tool-call sequence (Figure 2). For every base task, we proceed with the Evolution process through three LLM calls. Strategy analysis examines each write action to identify opportunities for adversarial confusion, drawing from manually curated catalog of patterns, e.g., demanding an action that the policy forbids, or the user mistakenly provides incorrect information (see Appendix B.3). Environment perturbation constructs decoy database records, entities similar to gold targets but with subtle disqualifying differences (e.g., flight on the same route with no available seats), which are merged into s0. Scenario rewriting rewrites the user instruction to exploit the adversarial opportunities. Each evolved task is re-validated; if validation fails, progressively simpler evolution variants are attempted before falling back to the base task (Appendix B.3). 6 Table 1: Main Results: Performance of agents on τ -Bench Verified (τ BV) and on our generated tasks (τ c-Bench), across three domains and two user simulators. For the airline domain, we additionally report pass^3 alongside the standard pass^1 scores. % denotes the relative change Oursτ BV τ BV 100.

sections.title: 4 Experimental Setup
sections.content: Datasets. We evaluate on and extend the three τ 2-Bench domains [9]: Airline, Retail, and Telecom (N = 50/114/114), using the τ -Bench-Verified corrected task sets [10]. Each domain models customer-support interaction in which simulated user issues request and the agent operates over domain-specific tools under defined policy. Telecom also provides tools to users. Since Telecom gold sequences encode only write actions, we restrict gold tool sequences to only write-type tools when applying TASTE and comparing τ c-Bench to τ 2-Bench. TASTE and τ c-Bench. We provide additional implementation details, including complete description of the hyperparameters in Appendix and Table 3. The prompts are described in Appendix D. We apply TASTE to each domain, using the original task counts. We train trigram model (n = 3) for 3,000 iterations and sample unique sequences whose lengths are drawn from skew-normal distribution with µ = 7, σ = 5, α = 2, clipped at maximum length of 15. The plausibility validator LLM is based on Gemini-3-Flash. For instantiating tasks, we also use Gemini-3-Flash, which also serves as the hint-assisted verifier agent. For task evolution, we use Gemini-3-Pro. Evaluated agents. We use the τ 2-Bench harness with default settings. We evaluate six agent LLMs (Gemini-3-Flash, Gemini-2.5-Flash, GPT-5.2, Qwen-32B, deepseek-3.1, Claude-Sonnet-4.6) paired with two user simulators (Gemini-3-Flash and GPT-5.2). All models use vendor-default thinking/reasoning effort settings; exact details are listed in Appendix B.4. Following Section 2, we report only pass^1 scores using final-state reward, and additionally report pass^3 for Airline. Costs. Stages 1 and 2 are cost-efficient, costing approximately $10 per domain. Stage 3 (task generation, evolution, and verification) costs approximately $2.50 per task. The total cost of generating τ c-Bench is $725. Evaluation costs vary by agentuser pair, with total cost of $520.

sections.title: 5 Results
sections.content: Main Results. The performance on our generated tasks is consistently and substantially lower against τ 2-Bench (Verified version, τ BV), with relative drops (%) ranging from 5% to 80%, depending on the agent and domain  (Table 1)  . We compared the performance of 11 agentuser pairs across three domains. The degradation is particularly striking for LLMs that appear to have nearly saturated τ BV: Gemini-3-flash, for instance, achieves 0.820.94 on τ BV across Airline, Retail, and Telecom domains (except Airline with Gemini-3-Flash user), yet drops to 0.280.61 on τ c-Bench. This suggests that the high τ BV scores may reflect benchmark saturation rather than robust task-solving ability. τ c-Bench exposes gaps that the original benchmark no longer captures, enabling differentiation among state-of-the-art LLMs and supporting continuous, scalable evaluation. 7 Figure 2: Coverage Metrics (Left): Weighted Edit Distance (WED) and Type-Token Ratio (TTR, averaged over {2, . . . , 6}), computed for both gold sequences (top) and sequences extracted from successful simulation trajectories (bottom). Airline Tool Frequency (Right): Distribution of tools in gold sequences. The τ BV distribution is notably more skewed. Other domains in Figure 5. Harder Tasks with Broader Coverage. We compare the coverage and diversity of τ c-Bench against τ BV, adopting n-gram diversity metrics from the NLP literature. In Figure 2 (left), we present two metrics. The first is the weighted edit distance (WED) between the tool sequences, capturing the structural dissimilarity of the required execution paths. The second is the Type-Token Ratio (TTR), the fraction of unique n-grams (n {2, ..., 6}) among all n-gram occurrences, capturing how much of the available tool-combination space is exercised. We also compute these metrics on tool sequences extracted from successful simulations (see Appendix A). Our tasks show consistent and substantial gains across all three domains: WED increases by up to 124%, TTR by up to 111%, and the tool-frequency distribution is markedly less skewed with entropy increased by 35% (Figure 2, right). These results indicate our tasks require agents to follow more varied and distinct execution paths. more detailed analysis (Appendix A) applies additional metrics; across all domains, metrics, and sequence sources, our tasks are consistently more challenging and diverse.

sections.title: 5.1 Method Analysis
sections.content: In this subsection, we analyze key design choices of TASTE and provide empirical justifications. Ablation of the Adaptive Contrastive n-gram Model. The goal of using n-gram model in TASTE is to sample from the space of valid tool sequences while maintaining broad coverage. We achieved 86.7% validity rate with our full trained model compared to uniform tool sampling (6.7%), illustrates the overall impact of our design. We extend the standard model in two ways: through adaptive online training and the integration of contrastive negative evidence. Figure 3 (left) presents an ablation study of the models components within the airline domain. Adaptive online training is essential: even when the model is initialized with n-grams from τ BV tasks, the validity rate without training reaches only 16.7%, whereas iterative training steadily improves it (as shown by the dark red line). The contrastive formulation provides complementary and substantial gain. Training with negative evidence (i.e., ingesting invalid n-grams into to avoid sampling them) improves the validity rate by 10 points at = 400, and by 20 points at = 800. Finally, evaluating the final model (k = 3000) trained with negative evidence to examine the impact of λneg on sampling reveals an additional 10-point improvement. In total, our design choices raise the validity rate from 6.7% to 86.7%. This 13 improvement enables us to sample large pool of plausibly valid candidates. Evaluation of the Verifier Agent. To validate generated tasks, we apply hint-assisted verifier agent, which attempts to complete each task given shuffled, partially masked list of gold tool calls. task is deemed valid if the agent succeeds. To evaluate its reliability, we construct validation dataset by comparing each original task from τ 2-Bench with its corresponding entry in τ -Bench-Verified , and assign these labels: valid: if the task was not modified in Verified; invalid: if the fix involved changing an action or substantially rewriting the instruction; tasks with minor fixes (e.g., capitalizing single word) are excluded. This yields 50 Airline tasks (8 invalid) and 86 Retail tasks (9 invalid). 8 Figure 3: n-gram Model (Left): Acceptance rate of sampled sequences across training stages and model configurations on the Airline domain. The dark red line traces the full adaptive contrastive model across iterations. Task Evolution (Right): Success rate of two agents (Gemini-3-Flash as the user simulator) on base vs. evolved tasks. Tasks are evolved by either GPT-5.2 or Gemini-3. The verifier agent achieves precision of 1.0 and 0.97 and recall of 0.75 and 0.83 in Airline and Retail, respectively. Accordingly, the agent may reject some valid tasks (recall < 0.9), but the near-perfect precision ensures any task passing is almost certainly valid. Finally, we also manually examined all tasks from τ c-Bench on which none of the evaluated agent-user pairs succeeded (1 Airline, 7 Retail, and 7 Telecom). All 15 tasks were found to be valid; failures were due to agent mistakes. Creating Difficult Tasks. TASTE increases task difficulty through task evolution. In Figure 3 (right), we compare base tasks in the Airline and Retail domains with evolved versions, using two agents and the Gemini-3-Flash user simulator. As shown, success rates are 3655% lower when tasks are evolved by Gemini-3, and 1637% lower when evolved by GPT-5.2, leading us to select Gemini-3. Difficulty can also be controlled through tool-sequence length and the read-to-write tool ratio during TASTEs sampling and selection stages. In Figure 4 (Appendix A), we compare two tiers of tasks (top 50% vs. bottom 50% by length and tool type ratio). As shown, performance on longer tasks is 30% lower on average (across all agent-user pairs and domains) and 43% lower on write-heavy tasks. This suggests that TASTE can be used to generate even harder tasks in the future, should existing benchmarks saturate.

sections.title: 6 Related Work
sections.content: Tool-use agent benchmarks vary along several axes. Early benchmarks evaluate single-turn tool selection and argument grounding over API collections [12, 13, 2224]. Recent work involves multi-turn interactions in persistent environments, such as web navigation and code repositories [14, 15]. Our focus is on conversational benchmarks that pair an agent with simulated user over mutable environment, and evaluate the resulting world state after the full interaction [8, 9, 25]. While valuable, existing agent benchmarks rely heavily on manually authored tasks, process that is costly, error-prone, and difficult to scale as agent capabilities advance [10, 11, 26]. Synthetic generation methods reduce annotation cost. Early approaches bootstrap instructions from seed sets [2729], while tool-use extensions generate gold tool calls through insertion filtering [1], synthesis from tool descriptions [12, 30], and post-hoc verification [31]. Recent pipelines scale to multi-turn using task blueprints, simulated environments, multi-agent role-playing, and trajectory feedback [3237]. Closest to our approach are ToolGrad [38] and Trajectory2Task [39], which synthesize tasks from trajectories. ToolGrad iteratively builds tool chains by proposing, executing, and selecting the best API call at each step, extending the sequence until completion before instantiating the user instruction. Trajectory2Task starts from an existing trajectory, progressively replaces tool calls, and evolves user instructions toward challenging intents. Both primarily employ reverse synthesis to increase the plausibility of generating valid task and to produce training data. Our motivation is different: we start from tool sequences to explicitly explore and cover the space of tool combinations, and our focus is on constructing better agent benchmarks rather than training data. We define coverage in terms of gold tool sequences, adopting diversity measures from the NLP literature [20, 4042] over tools rather than words. Existing benchmarks typically summarize diversity by domain, tool, or schema counts, and characterize difficulty through capability tags [12, 24, 25, 43]. Such summaries indicate which tools are available, but not whether tasks exercise diverse procedural 9 patterns of tool use. In contrast, sequence-level coverage allows us to measure whether benchmark explores broad range of agent behaviors rather than repeatedly testing similar patterns.

sections.title: 7 Conclusions
sections.content: In this work, we proposed TASTE, an automatic method for generating agent benchmark tasks that are difficult and cover broad range of tool-use patterns. Using TASTE, we constructed τ c-Bench, challenging extension of τ 2-Bench, and showed that models nearly saturating the original benchmark suffer performance drops on our generated tasks. Our focus was on conversational tool-using agents. Despite this focus, TASTE operates on tool sequences and environment specifications, making it readily adaptable to single-turn and non-conversational settings. Future work could apply TASTE to generate training data in addition to evaluation benchmarks, and extend the coverage and difficulty definitions to additional axes beyond those explored here.

Table 2 extends the results presented in Figure 2 to the full set of sequencelevel statistics: average length (mean number of tool calls per sequence); write/read balance (ratio of write tools to read and generic tools); within-cluster weighted edit distance (WED intra - mean pairwise distance over all sequence pairs in the cluster, indicating intra-set diversity); Shannon entropy at = 1 . . . 4 (n-gram occurrences are tallied with sliding window, normalised to probability distribution, then Hn = (cid:80) p(g) log2 p(g)) together with their vocabulary-normalised mean (Hn/(n log2 ) averaged over n, in [0, 1], allowing comparison across cells with different tool vocabularies); the count of unique n-grams at = 2 . . . 6 (compositional richness); and the mean type-token ratio (unique n-grams divided by total n-grams positions, averaged over = 2 . . . 6; higher means less repetition). The reported numbers compare against GoldSim; how we extract it is explained in the following paragraph. The GoldSim simulation set. The GoldSim columns aggregate agent behaviour into one representative tool sequence per task. Concretely, for given (domain, task-set) cell we proceed as follows: 1. Run pool. For every task i, gather the union of all simulation runs, pooled across agent, user simulator, and trial k. Filter unsuccessful runs (reward= 0.0). 2. Sequence extraction. For each run we read the agents tool calls in turn order and concatenate their identifiers into one ordered tool sequence (σ). 3. Per-task pick. If more then single candidate exist, we pick the candidate of minimal length. The intuition is that the shortest successful trajectory is the cleanest evidence of working solution path. 4. Gold fallback. If no candidate exists for task we use the tasks gold tool sequence (σ). This keeps the per-cell sequence count equal to the cells task count and avoids selection bias against hard tasks. 15 Figure 4: Mean accuracy on tasks bucketed by per-task golden-sequence length (left) and write-ratio (right), using µ 0.5σ thresholds with the middle band dropped. Difficulty Desiderata. Figure 4 validates the two structural axes we use to control task difficulty. Splitting tasks by per-task golden-sequence length produces 1320 pp accuracy gap between short and long buckets across all three domains. Splitting by per-task write-ratio produces 1631 pp gap between read-heavy and write-heavy buckets in airline and retail. Telecom is excluded from the write-ratio panel because its evaluation criteria encode only write actions, so per-task write-ratio collapses to 1.0 for every task. These results indicate that compositional length and write-heaviness act as genuine difficulty knobs rather than descriptive labels, supporting their use as control axes when sampling harder tasks. Tool-Usage Distributions Across Domains. Figure 5 reports complementary diversity views across the three τ 2-Bench domains. Each plot the per-tool relative frequency of the τ BV against τ c-Bench; the latter spreads usage more evenly across the tool vocabulary, raising the normalised entropy (H = Shannon entropy / log2 tools) reported in each legend. (a) retail (b) telecom (write actions only) Figure 5: Per-tool relative frequency on the retail and telecom domains, comparing τ BV against τ c-Bench; the airline counterpart is in Figure 2. Normalised entropy is reported in each panels legend; tools are ordered within each panel by τ BV task frequency. Telecom is restricted to write actions (21 of 43 tools, both distributions re-normalised over this subset) since the τ BV telecom tasks does not include read tools in their gold tool sequences. 16 Qualitative Comparison: Weighted vs. Regular Edit Distance. Figure 7 (Appendix C) contrasts k-medoids clustering on airline trajectories under our weighted edit distance (WED) and the unweighted edit distance (ED). Under WED (a), the three medoids correspond to clear, distinct userservice intents search/book/update, demand refund, and book and update and each medoids nearest neighbors stay close to it semantically: they share the medoids overall intent and only differ in surface-level details. Under ED (b), the medoids themselves remain interpretable, but the nearest neighbors drift away from the medoids intent, mixing in unrelated tool subsequences. Figure (c) makes the same point quantitatively: under WED the inter-medoid distances (3.664.66) are larger than the intra-cluster neighbor distances visible, whereas under ED interand intra-cluster scales are closer, reflecting weaker cluster separation. Together, these examples illustrate why we use WED rather than plain edit distance for the clustering step in Section 3.2: the type-aware substitution costs translate into clusters whose members are semantically coherent, not just superficially similar.

sections.title: B Implementation Details
sections.content: This appendix documents the end-to-end pipeline of TASTE (Section 3), as executed to produce τ c-Bench. The description is domain-agnostic: the only domain-specific resources are environment definition (policy document, tool specification, DB schema) and the set of original tasks. All three stages are executed as separate processes that communicate via artifacts; each stages output is the following stages input. Unless stated otherwise, every LLM call uses Gemini-3-flash with default LiteLLM3 settings; ablations over the model choice are reported in the Results section (Section 5). B.1 Stage 1: Tool Sequence Sampling with an Adaptive Contrastive n-gram Model Algorithm 1 summarizes the procedure described below. Model. The sampler instantiates the Adaptive Contrastive n-gram model of Section 3.1 with n=3 (trigram), Dirichlet smoothing λ0 = 0.1, and negative-evidence weight λneg = 1. Positive and negative count tables, + and , are initially empty. The available tokens set is the environments tool identifiers; two special tokens, BOS and EOS, are reserved for padding and termination and are never emitted by the sampler. Temperature schedule. The temperature schedule is instantiated as an exponential decay (k) = 1 + (T0 1) exp(cid:0)k/τdecay with T0 = 3.0 and τdecay = 1500. The iteration counter advances once per generation attempt. (cid:1), Length sampling. Candidate sequence lengths are drawn from skew-normal distribution with location 7, scale 5, and skewness 2.0; samples are hard-clipped to the integer range [1, 15] and rounded to the nearest integer. Generation. candidate action sequence is produced by prepending two BOS tokens to the empty sequence, then sampling tokens autoregressively from (ti ctxi) until the length drawn above is reached; Plausibility validator. Every candidate is evaluated by the plausibility validator: an LLM prompted with the domain-specific validation template (Box D.1). The prompt is templated with the candidate sequence and the domain environment. The validator determines whether the candidate sequence is valid and produces problematic indices if it is not. Ingestion. Sequences judged plausible are ingested into +: every n-gram window extracted from the BOS-padded sequence is incremented. Sequences judged implausible are ingested into : only windows whose final token index appears in problematic indices are incremented. Uniqueness is enforced by tuple-equality deduplication. Duplicate sequences are skipped without contributing to either table; the iteration counter still advances. 3https://www.litellm.ai/ 17 Seed initialization. Before the main loop, the gold tool sequence of the domains original tasks are each evaluated by the plausibility validator and ingested according to its verdict. Training loop. The loop runs for total of 3,000 generation attempts. The output of Stage 1 is the trained sampler with its final count tables. B.2 Stage 2: Clustering and Selection Algorithm 2 summarizes the procedure described below. Pool generation. pool of 2,000 unique tool sequences is drawn from the trained sampler. Sampling uses fixed temperature Tpool = 1.5 and the same skew-normal length distribution as in Stage 1. Weighted edit distance. Pairwise distances between sequences use standard Levenshtein recurrence with insertion and deletion cost 1 and hand-set tiered substitution cost: dsub(a, b) = 0 0.33 0.66 1. if = b, if type(a) = type(b) and group(a) = group(b), if type(a) = type(b) and group(a) = group(b), if type(a) = type(b). The type of tool is one of READ, WRITE, GENERIC, as declared in the tool specification; GENERIC tools are treated as READ for the type comparison only. The group of tool is the prefix of the tool name up to the first underscore (e.g., the group of search_direct_flight is search). Figure 7 (Appendix A) gives qualitative illustration of how this metric behaves on real airlinedomain sequences. For three clusters drawn from the validated = 50 medoid set with visibly distinct user-service intents (book new reservation; modify reservation flights; cancel reservation with refund certificate), Figure 7(a) shows that within-cluster distances stay between 1.0 and 3.98, while the inter-medoid matrix in Figure 7(b) reports smallest between-medoid distance of 5.31 so the cluster boundaries align with semantic intent, and cosmetic variants (read reordering, optional same-group write substitutions) are absorbed into the same cluster. K-medoids. The number of clusters is set to the number of seed tasks in the domain (50 for airline; 114 for retail and telecom). Medoids are initialized via the k-medoids++ scheme: the first medoid is drawn uniformly at random from the pool, and each subsequent medoid is drawn with probability proportional to the squared distance from the pool point to its nearest existing medoid. PAM-style update loop then alternates an assignment step (each point is assigned to its nearest medoid) with an update step (each medoid is replaced by the cluster member that minimizes total intra-cluster distance). The loop terminates when the medoid set stabilizes between consecutive iterations, with hard cap of 100 iterations. Medoid validation and replacement. Each medoid is validated by the same plausibility validator used in Stage 1. When medoid is judged implausible, replacement is searched among the other members of its cluster in order of increasing distance to the original medoid. If no member of the cluster is judged plausible, the cluster is marked unusable for this round. Re-clustering of unusable clusters. At the start of each re-clustering round, every medoid that has been accepted in previous round is frozen as an immovable center. fresh pool of sequences is drawn from the sampler under the same settings as the initial pool. Constrained k-medoids is then run with the frozen centers fixed and free centers (one per unusable cluster) selected from the new pool; the constraint is algorithmic rather than penalty-based: frozen indices never participate in the medoid-update step, but they remain in the nearest-medoid assignment. Newly selected free medoids are validated as above; unusable clusters remaining after three rounds are dropped, and the benchmark size is reduced accordingly. Output. The stage emits, for each plausible cluster, the medoid sequence. 18 B.3 Stage 3: Task Generation and Evolution Each plausible medoid σ from Stage 2 is instantiated as base task = (s0, u, σ) following Section 3.3: two LLM calls produce the task, which is then screened by multi-step validity pipeline with feedback-driven repair loop. The validated base task is then evolved into an adversarial variant by three-step pipeline with its own validity check. Algorithm 3 summarizes the procedure described below. Base task generation: scenario construction. The first LLM call (Box D.2) constructs coherent real-world scenario that motivates the tool sequence σ, inventing concrete entities and producing verbose, step-by-step user instruction u. Base task generation: database initialization. The second LLM call (Box D.3) produces the database entities required to execute σ. These entities are merged into the domains base environment state to form the tasks initial state s0. Validity checks. Following Section 3.3, the generated task is evaluated by pipeline combining rule-based and simulation-based checks; the pipeline short-circuits on the first failure. 1. (Rule) Arguments fully specified. 2. (Rule) Referenced entities exist in s0. 3. (Rule) s0 conforms to the domain schema. 4. (Rule) Gold tool-call sequence succeeds. 5. (LLM) Policy coherence review. An LLM reviews the task instructions against the policy (Box D.4). 6. (Simulation) Verifier agent. Described below. Verifier agent. The verifier agent of Section 3.3 is built on τ 2-Benchs LLMGTAgent workflow, which primes an LLM with the full list of gold tool calls (action hints) before running the task in τ 2-Benchs standard simulator. Running the simulation with fully-specified hints might be too easy: the agent can transcribe the tool calls into the conversation without engaging with the scenario, yields degenerate solvability check. Running it with no hints is too hard: competent agent may fail perfectly valid task because the task itself is difficult. We therefore deviate from the base implementation in two places, designed to leave enough scaffolding for the task to be solvable while forcing the agent to ground each decision in the conversation. We modify the hint list in two ways: First, we shuffle the order of the gold tool sequences. Second, for gold tool call with kwargs arguments, we randomly select kwargs arguments to drop, with = 0.3. We modify the instructions to the agent accordingly. task passes if the hinted-simulation yield reward of 1.0. Feedback-driven repair and cluster replacement. When any validity check fails, the pipeline invokes repair mechanism that feeds the failing steps error back to an LLM, which attempts to patch the offending part of the task or database. Tasks that cannot be repaired are regenerated from scratch; if repeated attempts fail, the medoid is replaced by another candidate from its cluster (Section 3.2). Task evolution: strategy analysis. Each validated base task is then evolved into harder variant. The first evolution LLM call (Box D.5) examines each WRITE action and selects global techniques from the catalog described below that shape the conversation as whole, defines conditional branches describing how the user reacts to different agent responses, and lists decoy entities to be constructed in the environment-perturbation step. Adversarial pattern catalog. We define catalog of adversarial patterns, each targeting distinct failure mode of tool-using agents. We divide the catalog into three main categories, each holding few applicable patterns. The strategy phase selects pattern per write action, based on the specific action and database context. 19 1. DB-grounded misdirection. The user leads the agent towards plausible but wrong argument values. careful agent catches these by looking things up in the DB. e.g., Decoy entity - where similar-but-wrong entity exists in the DB that plausibly matches the users vague description, with subtle disqualifier. 2. Policy Enforcement. The user pushes the agent toward actions that policy forbids, or beyond what the task requires. e.g., Eligibility Pressure - where user demands an action that policy forbids, such as ineligible refund. 3. Conversational adversity. The user controls the flow of information to stress-test the agents patience and robustness. e.g., Information withholding. Task evolution: environment perturbation. second LLM call materializes the designed techniques into concrete database records, which are merged into s0 additively. Task evolution: scenario rewriting. third LLM call (Box D.6) rewrites the user instruction to deploy the adversarial techniques from the strategy step and exploit the decoys from the environmentperturbation step, while keeping σ and its gold arguments unchanged. Evolved-task validation and graduated fallback. The evolved task passes through the validation pipeline mentioned above; Rule-based steps are skipped, as σ, its arguments, and the environment outside the decoys are preserved by construction. Failures at any evolution step trigger feedbackdriven retry with the failing steps error surfaced to the LLM. If full adversarial evolution still fails, the graduated fallback described below applies: lite rewrite is attempted without strategy analysis or decoys, and if it also fails the original base task is retained unchanged. Graduated fallback. When full adversarial evolution fails validation after multiple retry attempts, the pipeline applies lite fallback: simplified evolution that adds only mild adversarial elements without strategy analysis. If the lite version also fails, the original base task is retained unchanged. This graduated approach maximizes the fraction of tasks that receive some level of difficulty enhancement while ensuring that the final task set is always complete. Output. The stage emits set of tasks conforming to the τ 2-bench task schema, one per cluster, each in its evolved form. B.4 Consolidated Hyperparameter Table Table 3 reports the hyperparameters used in the reported runs; values are constant across domains unless noted. Table 4 lists the exact API identifiers behind every model name used in the paper. Table 3: Hyperparameters used in the reported runs.
